scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Lucian describes a social environment with senators and ex-consuls, which are distinctive institutions of the Roman imperial state during his lifetime.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Dated to the floruit of Lucian (c. 125–180 CE), who writes about contemporary social breaches in Roman elite spaces.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The mention of strigils, bath-houses, and obols (as a unit of account) reflects the Greco-Roman urban culture of the 2nd century CE.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Lucian is satirizing contemporary social affectations and slave-master dynamics during his floruit in the mid-2nd century CE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The Eleusinian Mysteries continued as a major religious institution of the Roman Empire, specifically within the province of Achaea.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Lucian refers to the contemporary practice and legal protections surrounding the sacred anonymity of Eleusinian officials in the 2nd century CE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The physical punishment of laborers and artisans is a characteristic feature of the stratified slave-holding society of the Roman East described by Lucian.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The text reflects contemporary 2nd-century CE social conditions where low-status workers were subject to corporal discipline.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The exercise of summary justice and physical restraint by local magistrates is consistent with provincial administration in the Roman Empire.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Lucian describes the contemporary powers of local officials to detain and fine individuals during his own lifetime.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The practice of erecting statues for athletic victors in city squares was a persistent feature of Greek civic life under Roman rule.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Lucian mentions a specific statue in a contemporary square as a reference point for his audience in the 2nd century CE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The curriculum of 'Second Sophistic' education (Thucydides, Plato, etc.) was the standard for elite social recognition in Lucian's world.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Lucian is advising on contemporary 2nd-century CE educational standards required for elite literary status.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The description reflects the domestic and social norms of the Hellenized upper classes in the Roman Empire.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Lucian describes the contemporary social treatment of women within elite households during his floruit.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Linguistic and gendered social stigmas are described as features of the contemporary Greek-speaking society of the Roman East.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Lucian critiques contemporary usage of derogatory gendered terms in the mid-2nd century CE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Zeuxis was a famous Greek painter of the late 5th/early 4th century BCE; the rule describes norms of that era.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The anecdote concerns the historical Zeuxis, active around 400 BCE, rather than Lucian's own time.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The text explicitly names Antiochus and his Macedonian contingent, referring to the Hellenistic Seleucid state.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The event refers to the 'Elephant Victory' of Antiochus I Soter, occurring around 275 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The mention of an 'Emperor' paying salaries for provincial and military administrators clearly identifies the Roman imperial system.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Lucian is discussing the contemporary 2nd-century CE reality of the Roman imperial civil service.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The professional practice of the Second Sophistic (sophists traveling to Gaul/the West for high fees) is a 2nd-century CE phenomenon.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Lucian refers to his own professional career and the fees he commanded during the mid-2nd century CE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The description of the Imperial cult (statues, temples, and shrines for the ruler) characterizes the Roman Empire.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Lucian describes the contemporary mechanism of honoring a Roman Emperor during his lifetime.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The text refers to the era of Solon and the Scythian philosopher Anacharsis in ancient Athens.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The verbatim anchors the rule to the life of Solon, who was Archon in -594 and associated with these legislative norms.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The narrative concerns Toxaris and the treatment of foreigners in Athens during the early 6th century BCE.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Lucian describes an archaic Athenian setting contemporary with Solon (-594).

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Solon is explicitly mentioned as being employed to frame laws for the city of Athens.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Refers to the legislative reforms of Solon in -594.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The text cites the examples of Solon, Pericles, and Aristides as the models for Athenian public oratory and nobility.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The verbatim evokes the height of the Athenian democracy, taking a representative midpoint of the lives of Pericles and Aristides.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Demades is an Athenian orator addressing the Athenian citizens during the late classical period.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The rules are articulated by Demades during his political career in the late 4th century BCE, specifically around the era of Macedonian ascendancy.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Demades describes the norms of the Athenian platform (bema) and the practice of public orators in the assembly.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Demades is documenting his own entry into and practice within Athenian public life in the late 4th century BCE.
